title: Die drei Schlangenblätter
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 16. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The sixteenth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 16), one of the darkest: a poor youth wins fame in war and marries a princess on the grim condition that whichever spouse dies first, the other be buried alive alongside. Entombed with her corpse, he watches a snake revive its slain fellow with three green leaves, and uses them to raise his wife — who repays him by throwing him into the sea with the ship’s skipper, until his faithful servant, keeper of the leaves, revives him again and the king delivers her to her death. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, thun/gethan/that, todt, Thüre, Thor, Muth, Noth, Athem, Vorrath, röthen and Schandthat, and the archaic preterite ward.

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein armer Mann, der konnte seinen einzigen Sohn nicht mehr ernähren.
sentences.l: There was once a poor man, who could no longer feed his only son.
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was a poor man who could no longer feed his only son.
sentences.n: The fixed fairy-tale opening “Es war einmal” = “once upon a time.” The relative “der konnte” resumes the man with a demonstrative der (‘he could’) — German often points back to a noun this way rather than with the plain relative.

sentences.t: Da sprach der Sohn „lieber Vater, es geht euch so kümmerlich, ich falle euch zur Last, lieber will ich selbst fortgehen und sehen wie ich mein Brot verdiene.“
sentences.l: Then the son spoke, “dear father, it goes so wretchedly for you, I fall to you as a burden; I would rather go away myself and see how I earn my bread.”
sentences.i: Then the son said, “Dear father, things go so hard for you, and I am a burden to you; I would rather go away myself and see how I can earn my bread.”
sentences.n: “es geht euch kümmerlich” — the impersonal “es geht + dative” means ‘things go (well/badly) for someone.’ euch is the old respectful ‘you’ the son uses to his father, a polite plural like French vous. “jemandem zur Last fallen” = to be a burden to someone. “lieber will ich” — lieber (‘rather’) plus wollen, ‘I would rather.’

sentences.t: Da gab ihm der Vater seinen Segen und nahm mit großer Trauer von ihm Abschied.
sentences.l: Then the father gave him his blessing and took leave of him with great grief.
sentences.i: Then the father gave him his blessing and, with great sorrow, took his leave of him.
sentences.n: “Abschied nehmen von” = to take leave of, say farewell to (Abschied here in its ‘leave-taking’ sense).

sentences.t: Zu dieser Zeit führte der König eines mächtigen Reichs Krieg, der Jüngling nahm Dienste bei ihm und zog mit ins Feld.
sentences.l: At this time the king of a mighty realm waged war; the youth took service with him and went along into the field.
sentences.i: At that time the king of a mighty realm was waging war; the young man took service with him and went off to the field with the rest.
sentences.n: “Krieg führen” = to wage war. “Reichs” is the genitive of Reich (‘of a realm’). “Dienste nehmen” = to take service, enlist. “zog mit ins Feld” — mitziehen (to go along) is separable; ‘into the field’ means off to war.

sentences.t: Und als er vor den Feind kam, so ward eine Schlacht geliefert, und es war große Gefahr, und regnete blaue Bohnen, daß seine Kameraden von allen Seiten niederfielen.
sentences.l: And when he came before the enemy, then a battle was delivered, and there was great danger, and it rained blue beans, so that his comrades fell down on all sides.
sentences.i: And when he came before the enemy, a battle was fought, and there was great peril, and blue beans rained down, so that his comrades fell on every side.
sentences.n: “eine Schlacht liefern” = to give / fight a battle; “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (modern wurde), here forming the passive ‘was fought’ — you meet ward constantly in Grimm. “blaue Bohnen” (‘blue beans’) is soldiers’ slang for musket balls, bullets. The old resumptive “als …, so …” (‘when …, then …’) recurs throughout. “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass.

sentences.t: Und als auch der Anführer blieb, so wollten die übrigen die Flucht ergreifen, aber der Jüngling trat heraus, sprach ihnen Muth zu und rief „wir wollen unser Vaterland nicht zu Grunde gehen lassen.“
sentences.l: And when the leader too fell, then the rest wanted to seize flight, but the youth stepped forth, spoke courage to them and called, “we will not let our fatherland go to ruin.”
sentences.i: And when the commander too was slain, the rest were about to take to flight, but the young man stepped forward, put heart into them, and cried, “We will not let our fatherland go to ruin.”
sentences.n: “blieb” — bleiben here in the old military sense ‘to fall, be killed’ (to ‘remain’ on the field). “die Flucht ergreifen” = to take flight. “jemandem Muth zusprechen” = to encourage, hearten someone (zusprechen is separable; Muth is the 1857 spelling of Mut). “zu Grunde gehen” = to be ruined, perish.

sentences.t: Da folgten ihm die andern, und er drang ein und schlug den Feind.
sentences.l: Then the others followed him, and he pressed in and beat the enemy.
sentences.i: Then the others followed him, and he broke through and defeated the enemy.
sentences.n: “folgten ihm” — folgen takes the dative: they followed him. “drang ein” — eindringen (to press in, penetrate) is separable; “schlug den Feind” — schlagen here ‘to beat, defeat.’

sentences.t: Der König, als er hörte daß er ihm allein den Sieg zu danken habe, erhob ihn über alle andern, gab ihm große Schätze und machte ihn zum ersten in seinem Reich.
sentences.l: The king, when he heard that he had to thank him alone for the victory, raised him above all others, gave him great treasures, and made him the first in his realm.
sentences.i: The king, when he heard that he owed the victory to him alone, raised him above all the others, gave him great treasures, and made him the foremost man in his realm.
sentences.n: “jemandem etwas zu danken haben” = to owe something to someone (‘to have to thank him for the victory’). In the reported clause the verb stands in subjunctive I: “zu danken habe” — the reported-speech subjunctive, everywhere in Grimm. “zum ersten machen” = to make the first, foremost.

sentences.t: Der König hatte eine Tochter, die war sehr schön, aber sie war auch sehr wunderlich.
sentences.l: The king had a daughter, she was very beautiful, but she was also very strange.
sentences.i: The king had a daughter who was very beautiful, but she was also very odd.
sentences.n: “die war” — again a demonstrative die resumes the daughter (‘she was’). wunderlich = strange, whimsical, odd — the tale is about to show just how.

sentences.t: Sie hatte das Gelübde gethan, keinen zum Herrn und Gemahl zu nehmen, der nicht verspräche, wenn sie zuerst stürbe, sich lebendig mit ihr begraben zu lassen.
sentences.l: She had made the vow to take none for lord and husband who would not promise, if she died first, to let himself be buried alive with her.
sentences.i: She had made a vow to take no man for lord and husband who would not promise, if she should die first, to let himself be buried alive with her.
sentences.n: “ein Gelübde thun” = to make a vow (thun is the 1857 spelling of tun). “zum Herrn und Gemahl nehmen” = to take as lord and husband. Both “verspräche” and “stürbe” are subjunctive II (versprechen, sterben) — the hypothetical ‘who would promise, if she were to die.’ The grim condition — being buried alive with the dead spouse — drives the whole tale; it echoes an old custom of entombing the living with the dead, treated here as a bride’s morbid test of love.

sentences.t: „Hat er mich von Herzen lieb,“ sagte sie, „wozu dient ihm dann noch das Leben?“
sentences.l: “If he loves me from the heart,” she said, “what use is life to him then?”
sentences.i: “If he loves me with all his heart,” she said, “what good is life to him then?”
sentences.n: “Hat er mich lieb, …” — a conditional made by putting the verb first (‘if he loves me, …’). “von Herzen lieb haben” = to love dearly, from the heart. “wozu dient ihm das Leben” = ‘to what does life serve him,’ i.e. what use is life to him (dienen + dative).

sentences.t: Dagegen wollte sie ein Gleiches thun, und wenn er zuerst stürbe, mit ihm in das Grab steigen.
sentences.l: In return she wanted to do a like thing, and if he died first, to climb into the grave with him.
sentences.i: In return she would do the same, and if he died first, go down into the grave with him.
sentences.n: “ein Gleiches thun” = to do the like, do the same. dagegen here = ‘in return, on the other hand.’ stürbe is again subjunctive II of sterben (‘if he were to die’).

sentences.t: Dieses seltsame Gelübde hatte bis jetzt alle Freier abgeschreckt, aber der Jüngling wurde von ihrer Schönheit so eingenommen, daß er auf nichts achtete, sondern bei ihrem Vater um sie anhielt.
sentences.l: This strange vow had until now scared off all suitors, but the youth was so taken with her beauty that he heeded nothing, but sued for her with her father.
sentences.i: This strange vow had until now frightened off all suitors, but the young man was so taken with her beauty that he heeded nothing, and asked her father for her hand.
sentences.n: “abschrecken” = to deter, scare off (past part. abgeschreckt, separable ab-). “eingenommen von” = taken with, captivated by. “auf nichts achten” = to pay attention to nothing (achten auf). “um jemanden anhalten” = to sue for someone’s hand, ask in marriage (anhalten is separable). “nicht …, sondern …” = ‘not …, but rather ….’

sentences.t: „Weißt du auch,“ sprach der König, „was du versprechen mußt?“
sentences.l: “Do you also know,” spoke the king, “what you must promise?”
sentences.i: “But do you know,” said the king, “what you must promise?”
sentences.n: “auch” here adds a note of ‘really, indeed’ to the question. “mußt” keeps the 1857 ß (modern musst).

sentences.t: „Ich muß mit ihr in das Grab gehen,“ antwortete er, „wenn ich sie überlebe, aber meine Liebe ist so groß, daß ich der Gefahr nicht achte.“
sentences.l: “I must go with her into the grave,” he answered, “if I outlive her; but my love is so great that I heed not the danger.”
sentences.i: “I must go into the grave with her,” he answered, “if I outlive her; but my love is so great that I take no heed of the danger.”
sentences.n: “überleben” = to outlive, survive. “der Gefahr nicht achten” — here achten takes the genitive (der Gefahr) in the old elevated construction, ‘to take no heed of the danger.’

sentences.t: Da willigte der König ein, und die Hochzeit ward mit großer Pracht gefeiert.
sentences.l: Then the king consented, and the wedding was celebrated with great splendor.
sentences.i: Then the king consented, and the wedding was celebrated with great splendour.
sentences.n: “einwilligen” = to consent, agree (separable ein-: willigte … ein). “ward … gefeiert” — ward again forming the passive (‘was celebrated’).

sentences.t: Nun lebten sie eine Zeitlang glücklich und vergnügt mit einander, da geschah es, daß die junge Königin in eine schwere Krankheit fiel, und kein Arzt ihr helfen konnte.
sentences.l: Now they lived for a while happily and contentedly with one another; then it happened that the young queen fell into a grave illness, and no physician could help her.
sentences.i: Now they lived happily and contentedly together for a while; then it came about that the young queen fell into a severe illness, and no doctor could help her.
sentences.n: “glücklich und vergnügt” is a stock formula, ‘happy and content.’ “in eine Krankheit fallen” = to fall ill. “kein Arzt … helfen konnte” — helfen takes the dative (ihr, ‘help her’).

sentences.t: Und als sie todt da lag, da erinnerte sich der junge König was er hatte versprechen müssen, und es grauste ihm davor, sich lebendig in das Grab zu legen, aber es war kein Ausweg: der König hatte alle Thore mit Wachen besetzen lassen, und es war nicht möglich dem Schicksal zu entgehen.
sentences.l: And when she lay there dead, then the young king remembered what he had had to promise, and he dreaded it, to lay himself alive into the grave; but there was no way out: the king had had all the gates manned with guards, and it was not possible to escape fate.
sentences.i: And when she lay there dead, the young king remembered what he had been made to promise, and he shuddered at laying himself alive into the grave; but there was no way out: the king had had all the gates set with guards, and it was not possible to escape his fate.
sentences.n: todt is the 1857 spelling of tot (dead), with the old -dt. “sich erinnern” + genitive/clause = to remember. “es grauste ihm davor” — impersonal grausen with the dative: ‘it filled him with dread.’ “besetzen lassen” = to have (something) manned/occupied — lassen for ‘to have done.’ “dem Schicksal entgehen” = to escape fate (entgehen + dative). Thore is the 1857 plural of Tor (gate), with old -th-.

sentences.t: Als der Tag kam, wo die Leiche in das königliche Gewölbe beigesetzt wurde, da ward er mit hinabgeführt, und dann das Thor verriegelt und verschlossen.
sentences.l: When the day came, when the corpse was laid to rest in the royal vault, then he was led down along with it, and then the gate bolted and locked.
sentences.i: When the day came on which the body was laid to rest in the royal vault, he was led down with it, and then the gate was bolted and locked.
sentences.n: “beisetzen” = to inter, lay to rest (separable bei-). “ward … hinabgeführt” — hinabführen (to lead down) is separable, and ‘mit’ = ‘along (with the corpse).’ Note how the closing verbs “verriegelt und verschlossen” carry over the ward of the passive from earlier in the sentence. Thor = gate (1857 -th-).

sentences.t: Neben dem Sarg stand ein Tisch, darauf vier Lichter, vier Laibe Brot und vier Flaschen Wein.
sentences.l: Beside the coffin stood a table, on it four candles, four loaves of bread, and four bottles of wine.
sentences.i: Beside the coffin stood a table, and on it four candles, four loaves of bread, and four bottles of wine.
sentences.n: “darauf” = ‘on it / thereon,’ pointing back to the table. Laibe is the plural of Laib (loaf). These four measures of light, bread and wine set the clock ticking on how long he can survive shut in.

sentences.t: Sobald dieser Vorrath zu Ende gieng, mußte er verschmachten.
sentences.l: As soon as this store came to an end, he had to languish away.
sentences.i: As soon as this supply came to an end, he was bound to perish of want.
sentences.n: sobald = as soon as. Vorrath (1857 spelling of Vorrat) = store, supply. gieng is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’) — this old -ie- recurs (gieng, giengen). verschmachten = to languish, waste away, pine (here, to die of hunger and thirst).

sentences.t: Nun saß er da voll Schmerz und Trauer, aß jeden Tag nur ein Bißlein Brot, trank nur einen Schluck Wein, und sah doch wie der Tod immer näher rückte.
sentences.l: Now he sat there full of pain and grief, ate every day only a little bite of bread, drank only a sip of wine, and yet saw how death moved ever nearer.
sentences.i: Now he sat there full of pain and sorrow, ate but a morsel of bread each day, drank only a sip of wine, and yet watched death drawing ever nearer.
sentences.n: Bißlein = a little bite, morsel (diminutive of Biß / Bissen, with the 1857 ß). “näher rücken” = to draw nearer (rücken, to move; separable sense with näher). Trauer = grief, mourning.

sentences.t: Indem er so vor sich hinstarrte, sah er aus der Ecke des Gewölbes eine Schlange hervor kriechen, die sich der Leiche näherte.
sentences.l: As he thus stared before himself, he saw out of the corner of the vault a snake creep forth, which drew near to the corpse.
sentences.i: As he sat staring before him, he saw a snake creep out of the corner of the vault and draw near the corpse.
sentences.n: indem here = ‘as, while’ (marking simultaneity). “vor sich hinstarren” = to stare before oneself (hinstarren, separable hin-). “hervor kriechen” = to creep forth (hervor, ‘out toward the front’). “sich nähern” = to draw near, approach (+ dative: der Leiche). This snake begins the tale’s magic core.

sentences.t: Und weil er dachte sie käme um daran zu nagen, zog er sein Schwert und sprach „so lange ich lebe sollst du sie nicht anrühren,“ und hieb sie in drei Stücke.
sentences.l: And because he thought it came to gnaw at it, he drew his sword and spoke, “as long as I live you shall not touch her,” and hewed it into three pieces.
sentences.i: And because he thought it had come to gnaw at her, he drew his sword and said, “As long as I live you shall not touch her,” and struck it into three pieces.
sentences.n: “käme” is subjunctive II of kommen, marking what he supposed (‘it was coming’). “um … zu nagen” = ‘in order to gnaw’ (nagen an, to gnaw at). anrühren = to touch (separable an-). “hieb … in drei Stücke” — hauen (preterite hieb) ‘to hew, strike.’ Note the pronoun shift: the snake is grammatically sie (die Schlange), but ‘sie … anrühren’ also plays on the dead wife.

sentences.t: Über ein Weilchen kroch eine zweite Schlange aus der Ecke hervor, als sie aber die andere todt und zerstückt liegen sah, gieng sie zurück, kam bald wieder und hatte drei grüne Blätter im Munde.
sentences.l: After a little while a second snake crept forth out of the corner, but when it saw the other lying dead and cut to pieces, it went back, soon came again, and had three green leaves in its mouth.
sentences.i: After a little while a second snake crept out of the corner, but when it saw the other lying dead and hacked apart, it went back, soon returned, and had three green leaves in its mouth.
sentences.n: “über ein Weilchen” = after a little while (Weilchen, diminutive of Weile). “hervor kriechen” again. zerstückt = cut to pieces, dismembered (prefix zer-, ‘apart, to bits’). “gieng zurück” — zurückgehen (to go back) is separable; gieng is the old spelling. Munde has the old dative -e. The three green leaves are the tale’s wonder: the revival-by-leaves motif is about to unfold.

sentences.t: Dann nahm sie die drei Stücke von der Schlange, legte sie, wie sie zusammen gehörten, und that auf jede Wunde eins von den Blättern.
sentences.l: Then it took the three pieces of the snake, laid them as they belonged together, and put onto each wound one of the leaves.
sentences.i: Then it took the three pieces of the snake, laid them together as they belonged, and put one of the leaves on each wound.
sentences.n: “wie sie zusammen gehörten” = ‘as they belonged together,’ i.e. fitting the pieces back into place. that is the 1857 spelling of tat (‘did, put,’ from thun). “eins von den Blättern” = one of the leaves (Blättern, dative plural of Blatt). Here the healing leaves knit the severed snake whole — watch what the man does with them next.

sentences.t: Alsbald fügte sich das Getrennte an einander, die Schlange regte sich und ward wieder lebendig, und beide eilten mit einander fort.
sentences.l: At once the severed parts joined themselves to one another, the snake stirred and became alive again, and both hurried away together.
sentences.i: At once the severed parts knit back together, the snake stirred and came alive again, and the two of them hurried away together.
sentences.n: alsbald = at once, presently (elevated). “sich an einander fügen” = to join together (fügen, to fit; ‘the severed thing,’ das Getrennte, is a nominalized past participle of trennen). “sich regen” = to stir, move. “ward wieder lebendig” = became alive again — ward once more. “eilten … fort” — forteilen (to hurry away) is separable.

sentences.t: Die Blätter blieben auf der Erde liegen, und dem Unglücklichen, der alles mit angesehen hatte, kam es in die Gedanken, ob nicht die wunderbare Kraft der Blätter, welche die Schlange wieder lebendig gemacht hatte, auch einem Menschen helfen könnte.
sentences.l: The leaves remained lying on the ground, and to the unfortunate one, who had watched it all, it came into his thoughts whether the wondrous power of the leaves, which had made the snake alive again, might not help a human being too.
sentences.i: The leaves were left lying on the ground, and it came into the unfortunate man’s mind, as he who had watched it all, whether the wondrous power of the leaves — which had brought the snake back to life — might not help a human being too.
sentences.n: “liegen bleiben” = to be left lying, remain (bleiben + infinitive). “der Unglückliche” = the unfortunate one, the wretch (nominalized adjective, here dative dem Unglücklichen). “mit ansehen” = to look on at, witness (separable an-: angesehen). “es kam ihm in die Gedanken, ob …” = ‘the thought came to him whether …’ Here the man reasons from the snake’s miracle to his own dead wife — the pivot of the tale.

sentences.t: Er hob also die Blätter auf und legte eins davon auf den Mund der Todten, die beiden andern auf ihre Augen.
sentences.l: So he picked up the leaves and laid one of them onto the mouth of the dead woman, the two others onto her eyes.
sentences.i: So he picked up the leaves and laid one of them on the dead woman’s mouth, the other two on her eyes.
sentences.n: “hob … auf” — aufheben (to pick up) is separable. “der Todten” = of the dead woman (nominalized adjective, genitive; 1857 spelling of Toten). He applies the leaves to mouth and eyes just as the snake did to the wounds.

sentences.t: Und kaum war es geschehen, so bewegte sich das Blut in den Adern, stieg in das bleiche Angesicht und röthete es wieder.
sentences.l: And scarcely had it happened, then the blood stirred in the veins, rose into the pale face, and reddened it again.
sentences.i: And scarcely was it done when the blood stirred in her veins, rose into her pale face, and reddened it once more.
sentences.n: “kaum … war es geschehen, so …” = ‘scarcely had it happened, when …’ (the verb-first kaum-clause). Adern is the plural of Ader (vein). Angesicht = countenance, face (elevated). röthete is the 1857 spelling of rötete (röthen / röten, to redden).

sentences.t: Da zog sie Athem, schlug die Augen auf und sprach „ach, Gott, wo bin ich?“
sentences.l: Then she drew breath, struck her eyes open, and spoke, “ah, God, where am I?”
sentences.i: Then she drew breath, opened her eyes, and said, “Oh God, where am I?”
sentences.n: “Athem ziehen” = to draw breath (Athem is the 1857 spelling of Atem). “die Augen aufschlagen” = to open one’s eyes (aufschlagen, separable auf-). The revival is complete — the leaves have worked on a human being.

sentences.t: „Du bist bei mir, liebe Frau,“ antwortete er, und erzählte ihr wie alles gekommen war und er sie wieder ins Leben erweckt hatte.
sentences.l: “You are with me, dear wife,” he answered, and told her how everything had come about and he had awakened her back into life.
sentences.i: “You are with me, dear wife,” he answered, and told her how it had all come about, and how he had brought her back to life.
sentences.n: “wieder ins Leben erwecken” = to awaken back to life, revive (erwecken). Frau here in the older sense ‘wife.’

sentences.t: Dann reichte er ihr etwas Wein und Brot, und als sie wieder zu Kräften gekommen war, erhob sie sich, und sie giengen zu der Thüre, und klopften und riefen so laut daß es die Wachen hörten und dem König meldeten.
sentences.l: Then he handed her some wine and bread, and when she had come to her strength again, she raised herself up, and they went to the door, and knocked and called so loud that the guards heard it and reported it to the king.
sentences.i: Then he handed her some wine and bread, and when she had regained her strength, she rose, and they went to the door and knocked and called so loudly that the guards heard it and reported it to the king.
sentences.n: “zu Kräften kommen” = to gain / regain strength. “sich erheben” = to rise up. giengen is the 1857 plural of gingen; Thüre = door (1857 -th-, modern Tür). Wachen = the guards (die Wache). “dem König meldeten” — melden + dative, to report to the king.

sentences.t: Der König kam selbst herab und öffnete die Thüre, da fand er beide frisch und gesund, und freute sich mit ihnen daß nun alle Noth überstanden war.
sentences.l: The king came down himself and opened the door; there he found both fresh and healthy, and rejoiced with them that now all distress was overcome.
sentences.i: The king came down himself and opened the door, and there he found the two of them fresh and well, and rejoiced with them that all the distress was now over.
sentences.n: “kam … herab” — herabkommen (to come down) is separable. “sich freuen” = to rejoice, be glad. Noth is the 1857 spelling of Not (need, distress). “überstehen” = to get through, weather, come safely out of (past part. überstanden).

sentences.t: Die drei Schlangenblätter aber nahm der junge König mit, gab sie einem Diener und sprach „verwahr sie mir sorgfältig, und trag sie zu jeder Zeit bei dir, wer weiß in welcher Noth sie uns noch helfen können.“
sentences.l: But the three snake-leaves the young king took along, gave them to a servant, and spoke, “keep them safe for me carefully, and carry them at all times upon you; who knows in what distress they may yet help us.”
sentences.i: But the three snake-leaves the young king took with him, gave them to a servant, and said, “Keep them safe for me with care, and carry them on you at all times; who knows in what need they may yet help us.”
sentences.n: “nahm … mit” — mitnehmen (to take along) is separable. verwahr and trag are imperatives (verwahren, to keep safe, guard; tragen, to carry). “verwahr sie mir” — the mir is a dative of interest, ‘keep them safe for me.’ This careful stewardship of the leaves is exactly what will save the king’s life later.

sentences.t: Es war aber in der Frau, nachdem sie wieder ins Leben war erweckt worden, eine Veränderung vorgegangen: es war als ob alle Liebe zu ihrem Manne aus ihrem Herzen gewichen wäre.
sentences.l: But in the woman, after she had been awakened back into life, a change had come over her: it was as though all love for her husband had departed from her heart.
sentences.i: But in the woman, after she had been brought back to life, a change had come over her: it was as though all love for her husband had vanished from her heart.
sentences.n: “eine Veränderung war vorgegangen” = a change had taken place, come over (vorgehen, to occur; separable vor-). “war erweckt worden” = had been awakened (passive perfect). “als ob … gewichen wäre” — als ob (‘as if’) takes subjunctive II: weichen (to yield, give way, depart) → “gewichen wäre.” Her cured body hides a poisoned heart — the tale’s dark turn.

sentences.t: Als er nach einiger Zeit eine Fahrt zu seinem alten Vater über das Meer machen wollte und sie auf ein Schiff gestiegen waren, so vergaß sie die große Liebe und Treue, die er ihr bewiesen und womit er sie vom Tode gerettet hatte, und faßte eine böse Neigung zu dem Schiffer.
sentences.l: When after some time he wanted to make a voyage over the sea to his old father, and they had boarded a ship, then she forgot the great love and faithfulness that he had shown her and wherewith he had saved her from death, and conceived a wicked inclination toward the boatman.
sentences.i: When, after some time, he wished to make a voyage across the sea to his old father, and they had boarded a ship, she forgot the great love and faithfulness he had shown her, and by which he had saved her from death, and conceived a wicked fondness for the skipper.
sentences.n: Fahrt = journey, voyage. “auf ein Schiff steigen” = to board a ship (gestiegen, past part. of steigen). Treue = faithfulness, loyalty; “bewiesen” — beweisen here ‘to show, display.’ “vom Tode retten” = to save from death (Tode has the old dative -e). “eine Neigung zu jemandem fassen” = to conceive a fondness/inclination for someone. Schiffer = boatman, skipper.

sentences.t: Und als der junge König einmal da lag und schlief, rief sie den Schiffer herbei, und faßte den schlafenden am Kopfe, und der Schiffer mußte ihn an den Füßen fassen, und so warfen sie ihn hinab ins Meer.
sentences.l: And when the young king once lay there and slept, she called the boatman hither, and grasped the sleeping man at the head, and the boatman had to grasp him at the feet, and so they threw him down into the sea.
sentences.i: And once, when the young king lay there asleep, she called the skipper to her, and took the sleeping man by the head, and the skipper had to take him by the feet, and so they threw him down into the sea.
sentences.n: “herbeirufen” = to call over, summon (separable herbei-). fassen = to grasp, take hold of; “am Kopfe … an den Füßen” — the old dative -e on Kopf. “den schlafenden” = the sleeping one (nominalized participle). The murder is plain and unsoftened — Grimm does not spare the drowning.

sentences.t: Als die Schandthat vollbracht war, sprach sie zu ihm „nun laß uns heimkehren und sagen er sei unterwegs gestorben.
sentences.l: When the shameful deed was accomplished, she spoke to him, “now let us return home and say he died on the way.
sentences.i: When the foul deed was done, she said to him, “Now let us return home and say that he died on the way.
sentences.n: Schandthat = shameful deed, crime (1857 spelling of Schandtat). vollbringen = to accomplish, carry out (past part. vollbracht). heimkehren = to return home (separable heim-). “er sei … gestorben” — subjunctive I (sein) marks the reported lie she plans to tell: ‘that he died,’ as reported, not as fact.

sentences.t: Ich will dich schon bei meinem Vater so herausstreichen und rühmen, daß er mich mit dir vermählt und dich zum Erben seiner Krone einsetzt.“
sentences.l: I will surely praise you up and extol you so before my father that he weds me to you and installs you as heir of his crown.”
sentences.i: I will so sing your praises and extol you before my father that he will marry me to you and make you heir to his crown.”
sentences.n: schon here is reassuring, ‘surely, don’t worry.’ herausstreichen = to praise up, sing the praises of (separable heraus-); rühmen = to extol, laud. “zum Erben einsetzen” = to appoint / install as heir (einsetzen, separable ein-; Erben, dative of Erbe). Her scheme even names the reward she offers her accomplice.

sentences.t: Aber der treue Diener, der alles mit angesehen hatte, machte unbemerkt ein kleines Schifflein von dem großen los, setzte sich hinein, schiffte seinem Herrn nach, und ließ die Verräther fortfahren.
sentences.l: But the faithful servant, who had watched it all, unnoticed made a little boat loose from the big one, set himself in it, sailed after his master, and let the traitors travel on.
sentences.i: But the faithful servant, who had watched it all, quietly loosed a little boat from the big ship, got into it, sailed after his master, and let the traitors go on their way.
sentences.n: “mit ansehen” = to witness, look on at (again). “losmachen” = to loose, cast off (separable los-: machte … los). Schifflein = little boat, skiff (diminutive of Schiff). “nachschiffen” = to sail after (separable nach-: schiffte … nach; ‘seinem Herrn’ dative). Verräther = traitors (1857 spelling of Verräter). fortfahren = to travel / sail on (separable fort-).

sentences.t: Er fischte den Todten wieder auf, und mit Hilfe der drei Schlangenblätter, die er bei sich trug, und auf die Augen und den Mund legte, brachte er ihn glücklich wieder ins Leben.
sentences.l: He fished the dead man up again, and with the help of the three snake-leaves, which he carried upon himself and laid onto the eyes and the mouth, he brought him happily back into life.
sentences.i: He fished the dead man up again, and with the help of the three snake-leaves, which he carried on him and laid on the eyes and the mouth, he happily brought him back to life.
sentences.n: “auffischen” = to fish up, out (separable auf-: fischte … auf). den Todten = the dead man (nominalized adjective, accusative). Here the leaves work their revival a second time — the servant’s faithful keeping of them (from the crypt scene) pays off exactly as the king foresaw. glücklich here = ‘happily, successfully.’

sentences.t: Sie ruderten beide aus allen Kräften Tag und Nacht, und ihr kleines Schiff flog so schnell dahin daß sie früher als das andere bei dem alten Könige anlangten.
sentences.l: They both rowed with all their strength day and night, and their little ship flew along so fast that they arrived at the old king’s sooner than the other.
sentences.i: The two of them rowed with all their might day and night, and their little boat sped along so fast that they reached the old king sooner than the other ship.
sentences.n: “aus allen Kräften” = with all one’s strength. “dahinfliegen” = to fly / speed along (separable dahin-: flog … dahin). früher = sooner, earlier (comparative of früh). “anlangen” = to arrive, reach (separable an-; ‘bei dem alten Könige,’ with old dative -e on König).

sentences.t: Er verwunderte sich als er sie allein kommen sah und fragte was ihnen begegnet wäre.
sentences.l: He wondered when he saw them come alone and asked what had happened to them.
sentences.i: He was astonished when he saw them come alone, and asked what had befallen them.
sentences.n: “sich verwundern” = to wonder, be astonished. “was ihnen begegnet wäre” — begegnen (to happen to, befall; + dative ihnen) in subjunctive II (wäre), marking the reported question.

sentences.t: Als er die Bosheit seiner Tochter vernahm, sprach er „ich kanns nicht glauben, daß sie so schlecht gehandelt hat, aber die Wahrheit wird bald an den Tag kommen,“ und hieß beide in eine verborgene Kammer gehen und sich vor jedermann heimlich halten.
sentences.l: When he learned of his daughter’s wickedness, he spoke, “I cannot believe it, that she has acted so badly, but the truth will soon come to the day,” and bade both go into a hidden chamber and keep themselves secret from everyone.
sentences.i: When he heard of his daughter’s wickedness, he said, “I cannot believe she has acted so basely, but the truth will soon come to light,” and bade the two of them go into a hidden chamber and keep themselves concealed from everyone.
sentences.n: vernehmen = to hear, learn, perceive (elevated). kanns = kann es (‘can it’), a contraction. “schlecht handeln” = to act badly, wickedly (gehandelt, past part. of handeln). “an den Tag kommen” = to come to light. “hieß … gehen” — heißen + infinitive = to bid, order someone to do. “sich heimlich halten” = to keep oneself hidden.

sentences.t: Bald hernach kam das große Schiff herangefahren, und die gottlose Frau erschien vor ihrem Vater mit einer betrübten Miene.
sentences.l: Soon thereafter the big ship came sailing up, and the godless woman appeared before her father with a sorrowful mien.
sentences.i: Soon afterward the big ship came sailing up, and the wicked woman appeared before her father with a mournful expression.
sentences.n: “hernach” = thereafter, afterward. “kam herangefahren” = came sailing/driving up (heranfahren, separable heran-; the past participle after kommen expresses the manner of arrival, ‘came ~ing’). gottlos = godless, wicked. Miene = mien, expression (not to be confused with meine, ‘my’).

sentences.t: Er sprach „warum kehrst du allein zurück? wo ist dein Mann?“
sentences.l: He spoke, “why do you return alone? where is your husband?”
sentences.i: He said, “Why do you come back alone? Where is your husband?”
sentences.n: “zurückkehren” = to return, come back (separable zurück-: kehrst … zurück).

sentences.t: „Ach, lieber Vater,“ antwortete sie, „ich komme in großer Trauer wieder heim, mein Mann ist während der Fahrt plötzlich erkrankt und gestorben, und wenn der gute Schiffer mir nicht Beistand geleistet hätte, so wäre es mir schlimm ergangen; er ist bei seinem Tode zugegen gewesen und kann euch alles erzählen.“
sentences.l: “Oh, dear father,” she answered, “I come home again in great grief; my husband suddenly fell ill during the voyage and died, and if the good boatman had not rendered me assistance, then it would have gone badly for me; he was present at his death and can tell you everything.”
sentences.i: “Oh, dear father,” she answered, “I come home in great grief; my husband suddenly took ill during the voyage and died, and if the good skipper had not lent me his aid, it would have gone badly for me; he was present at his death and can tell you everything.”
sentences.n: erkranken = to fall ill (erkrankt). “Beistand leisten” = to give aid, lend assistance (geleistet). “hätte … geleistet … so wäre … ergangen” — subjunctive II throughout for the counterfactual: ‘if he had not …, it would have gone …’; ergehen is impersonal with the dative (es ergeht mir schlimm, ‘it goes badly for me’). zugegen sein = to be present. Every word of her account is a lie.

sentences.t: Der König sprach „ich will den Todten wieder lebendig machen“ und öffnete die Kammer, und hieß die beiden heraus gehen.
sentences.l: The king spoke, “I will make the dead man alive again,” and opened the chamber, and bade the two come out.
sentences.i: The king said, “I will bring the dead man back to life,” and opened the chamber, and bade the two of them come out.
sentences.n: den Todten = the dead man (nominalized adjective, accusative). “lebendig machen” = to make alive, bring to life. “hieß … heraus gehen” — heißen + infinitive (‘bade them go out’); herausgehen is separable. The king turns her own lie into the trap that exposes her.

sentences.t: Die Frau, als sie ihren Mann erblickte, war wie vom Donner gerührt, sank auf die Knie und bat um Gnade.
sentences.l: The woman, when she caught sight of her husband, was as if touched by thunder, sank onto her knees, and begged for mercy.
sentences.i: The woman, when she caught sight of her husband, was as though thunderstruck, sank to her knees, and begged for mercy.
sentences.n: erblicken = to catch sight of, behold. “wie vom Donner gerührt” = as if struck by thunder, thunderstruck (Donner, thunder; a set phrase). “auf die Knie sinken” = to sink to one’s knees (Knie, pl.). “um Gnade bitten” = to beg for mercy.

sentences.t: Der König sprach „da ist keine Gnade, er war bereit mit dir zu sterben und hat dir dein Leben wieder gegeben, du aber hast ihn im Schlaf umgebracht, und sollst deinen verdienten Lohn empfangen.“
sentences.l: The king spoke, “there is no mercy; he was ready to die with you and has given you your life again, but you have killed him in his sleep, and shall receive your deserved reward.”
sentences.i: The king said, “There is no mercy here; he was ready to die with you and gave you back your life, but you killed him in his sleep, and you shall receive your just deserts.”
sentences.n: “umbringen” = to kill, do to death (past part. umgebracht). “der verdiente Lohn” = the deserved reward, just deserts (verdient, past part. of verdienen used as adjective). “empfangen” = to receive. The king’s sentence is the tale’s bleak justice.

sentences.t: Da ward sie mit ihrem Helfershelfer in ein durchlöchertes Schiff gesetzt und hinaus ins Meer getrieben, wo sie bald in den Wellen versanken.
sentences.l: Then she was set with her accomplice into a ship full of holes and driven out into the sea, where they soon sank beneath the waves.
sentences.i: Then she was put, together with her accomplice, into a ship riddled with holes and driven out to sea, where they soon sank beneath the waves.
sentences.n: “ward … gesetzt … und getrieben” — ward again forming the passive (‘was set … and driven’). Helfershelfer = accomplice, henchman (literally ‘helper’s helper’). durchlöchert = full of holes, riddled (from durchlöchern, to perforate). “in den Wellen versinken” = to sink beneath the waves (versanken, 3rd pl. preterite of versinken). The execution closes the tale as darkly as it began.
